food_name: Ayam Geprek (Spicy Smashed Fried Chicken with Chilli Sambal, Level 8)
aliases: Smashed Fried Chicken with Sambal Level 8

english: Smashed Fried Chicken with Sambal Level 8

food_subgroup: Fried chicken with spicy chilli sambal (ayam geprek)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ayam geprek. FOODON_03302757 (fried chicken food product) is used as the closest parent. The sambal chilli component is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. Ayam geprek as a composite dish with sambal is not individually catalogued in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for ayam geprek or Indonesian smashed fried chicken with sambal.

serving.description: 1 serving (1 piece of smashed fried chicken with sambal)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of ayam geprek is approximately 250 g, comprising one piece of battered deep-fried chicken smashed and mixed with a generous portion of fresh chilli sambal.

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Goreng (Fried Chicken) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for sambal chilli component
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ayam geprek is not individually listed in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for battered deep-fried chicken (NDB 05062, Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter) with FAO/INFOODS SEA values for fresh chilli sambal. The composite reflects approximately 80% fried chicken and 20% sambal by weight. Values are best estimates and should be verified against a laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried chicken with batter coating — analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken and white rice composite; University of Sydney GI Database reference for fried battered foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ayam geprek in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated based on the battered wheat-flour coating (primary glycemic driver) using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for battered fried chicken products (GI ~65, medium category). The sambal chilli component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 28.0) / 100 = 18.2, using 250 g serving size and 11.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per serving) from seasoned batter, frying oil, and sambal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit ayam geprek to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single serving may contribute approximately 46% of the WHO-recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 65) driven by wheat-flour batter coating; moderate carbohydrate load per serving (~28 g carbohydrate, GL ~18)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the medium GI and moderate glycemic load of ayam geprek; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and avoiding additional rice or starchy sides can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (14.8 g/100 g) and saturated fat (3.9 g/100 g) from deep-frying in oil, plus high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of ayam geprek consumption due to its combined saturated fat and sodium burden; choosing grilled chicken alternatives and requesting less oil in the sambal preparation is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek (Spicy Smashed Fried Chicken with Chilli Sambal, Level 8) is a popular Indonesian street-food dish consisting of a piece of battered, deep-fried chicken that is smashed or pressed flat and then mixed or topped with a freshly pounded spicy sambal made from raw red chillies, garlic, and oil. The 'Level 8' designation indicates an extremely high chilli intensity, placing this dish among the spiciest variants of ayam geprek available. The dish originated in Yogyakarta, Indonesia, and has spread widely across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, where it is served by Indonesian-cuisine restaurants and food-delivery platforms. The texture contrasts a crispy, golden batter exterior with juicy chicken meat, while the sambal delivers sharp, fiery heat with aromatic garlic notes. Nutritionally, ayam geprek is a moderate-to-high calorie dish (~249 kcal/100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~18.5 g/100 g) from the chicken, significant fat from deep-frying (~14.8 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from the wheat-flour batter (~11.2 g/100 g). Sodium content is high (~420 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven primarily by the batter coating. The fresh chilli sambal contributes vitamin C and capsaicin, which may have mild anti-inflammatory properties. Clinically, this dish warrants moderation for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and glycemic load profile.
common_user_queries: How spicy is ayam geprek level 8?
What is the GI of fried chicken with sambal?

confidence_reason: Ayam geprek as a specific dish is not individually listed in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for battered deep-fried chicken with FAO/INFOODS SEA sambal chilli values. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried chicken products. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates: approximately 80% battered deep-fried chicken (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062) and 20% fresh chilli sambal (FAO/INFOODS SEA). Laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant product would be required for high-confidence values. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category and is not expected to be a significant contributor in fried chicken with sambal. 3. The GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken products in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for ayam geprek was found. 4. Sodium estimate (~420 mg/100 g) reflects seasoned batter and sambal; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 5. The input record listed cuisine_tags including 'Stuffed Pancake', 'Pancake', and 'Coffee', which appear to be restaurant-level tags from Smile Martabak - Funan (a martabak restaurant) rather than dish-level descriptors for ayam geprek; these tags have been disregarded for nutritional and ontological classification. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the Indonesian Muslim culinary tradition of ayam geprek; verification of the specific restaurant's halal certification is recommended. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy composite and FAO/INFOODS SEA data; vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database.
